questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: A circle is the locus of points
questions.question: What is the set of all points in a plane that are at a fixed distance from a given point called?
questions.hint: Recall the definition based on equal distances from a center.
questions.explanation: A circle is defined as the locus of points equidistant from a fixed point called the center. Option A describes an ellipse which has two foci, not a single fixed distance from one point.

source_trace.structural_element: A circle is the locus of points
source_trace.content_anchor: set of points each of which is equidistant from a fixed point

explanations_tiered.definition: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at a constant distance (radius) from a fixed point (center).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The circle is defined purely by distance equality from one central point. Any point on the circle satisfies this condition, and any point inside or outside does not.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a circle like a rope tied to a post: every point where the rope can reach is on the circle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In geometry, a circle is the locus of points equidistant from the center \(O\) with distance \(r\). The equation is \(x^2 + y^2 = r^2\) in coordinate form.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally, a circle is a 1-dimensional manifold embedded in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) defined by \(\{P : |P-O| = r\}\). The constant-distance property gives rotational symmetry.
reasoning_steps: Identify the fixed point as the center.
Identify the fixed distance as the radius.
Recognise that all points satisfying this form a circle.
misconceptions: A circle is sometimes confused with a disc — the circle is only the boundary, not the interior.
Some think a circle has a finite number of points, but it contains infinitely many points.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Circle: all points equidistant from a center.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of all points in a plane at a given distance from a fixed point.

flashcard.back: A circle is the set of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed point called the center.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the mathematical definition of a circle?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A circle is the set of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed center point.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The locus of points in a plane that are at a constant distance from a given point.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse circle with disc — clarify boundary vs area.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the symmetrical properties of circles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A circle is the set of points at fixed distance from a fixed point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A circle has center \(O\) and chord \(PQ\) with midpoint \(M\). What is the relationship between line \(OM\) and chord \(PQ\)?
questions.hint: Theorem 5.1 states joining center to midpoint of a chord creates a right angle.
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.1 states the line from the center to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord. Option B confuses length equality with perpendicularity, which is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: line segment joining center to midpoint of chord is perpendicular to chord

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.1: The line from the circle's center to the midpoint of any chord is perpendicular to that chord.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This theorem links chord geometry with symmetry: the perpendicular from center bisects the chord; conversely, joining center to midpoint gives perpendicular.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you draw a line from the middle of the circle to the middle of a chord, it makes a perfect right angle with the chord.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given circle center \(O\) and chord \(PQ\) with midpoint \(M\), then \(OM \perp PQ\). This follows from symmetry about diameter through \(M\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: By congruent triangles \(\triangle OMP \cong \triangle OMQ\) (SSS), \(\angle OMP = \angle OMQ = 90^\circ\). The perpendicular bisector property holds.
misconceptions: Some students think the line from center always bisects the chord without perpendicularity — Theorem 5.1 gives both.
Confusing with Theorem 5.2: both are related but different given conditions.

glossary_entry.definition: A line segment whose endpoints lie on the circle.

flashcard.front: What is the angle between the line from center to chord midpoint and the chord?

visual_system.visual_description: A circle with center O, chord PQ horizontal, midpoint M, segment OM vertical.
visual_system.caption: Line from center to chord midpoint is perpendicular.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle center O, chord PQ, M midpoint, OM perpendicular to PQ.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does Theorem 5.1 say about center, chord midpoint?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The line from center to chord midpoint is perpendicular to the chord.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The segment from circle center to midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use folding along diameter to prove symmetry.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use symmetrical properties of circles to solve problems
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Theorem 5.1 directly states OM ⟂ PQ.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a circle with center \(O\), a perpendicular line from \(O\) is drawn to chord \(AB\) meeting at point \(N\). What does Theorem 5.2 guarantee about \(N\)?

options.A: \(N\) is the center of the circle

questions.hint: Theorem 5.2 is the converse of Theorem 5.1: perpendicular from center bisects chord.
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.2 states a perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord, so \(N\) is the midpoint of \(AB\). Option A confuses \(N\) with center \(O\), which is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: line from center perpendicular to chord bisects the chord

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.2: A line drawn from the center of a circle perpendicular to a chord bisects that chord.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This theorem complements Theorem 5.1: perpendicularity from center implies the chord is cut into two equal halves at the foot of the perpendicular.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drop a straight-down line from the circle's middle onto a chord, that line cuts the chord exactly in half.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given \(O\) center, chord \(AB\), and \(ON \perp AB\) at \(N\), then \(AN = NB\). Proof by RHS congruence of \(\triangle AON\) and \(\triangle BON\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: By RHS congruence (right angle, \(OA=OB\) radii, common side \(ON\)), triangles \(AON\) and \(BON\) are congruent, so \(AN = NB\).
misconceptions: Students think any line from center to chord bisects it — only perpendicular ones bisect.
Confusing with median in triangles, but this is specific to circles.

glossary_entry.definition: Perpendicular from circle center to a chord bisects the chord.

flashcard.front: If a line from the circle center is perpendicular to a chord, what does it do to the chord?
flashcard.back: It bisects the chord (cuts it into two equal segments).

visual_system.visual_description: Circle center O, chord AB horizontal, perpendicular ON vertical meeting chord at N.
visual_system.caption: Perpendicular from center meets chord at its midpoint.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle O, chord AB, perpendicular ON, N midpoint of AB.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is Theorem 5.2 in circle geometry?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A line from center perpendicular to chord cuts chord into two equal parts.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use RHS congruence to prove it formally.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use symmetrical properties of circles to solve problems
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Theorem 5.2 guarantees N is midpoint of AB.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Two chords \(AB\) and \(CD\) in the same circle have equal length. What must be true about their distances from the center \(O\)?

options.C: One distance is twice the other
options.D: The sum of distances equals the radius

questions.hint: Equal chords are equidistant from the center (Theorem 5.3).
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.3 states that equal chords are equidistant from the center. Option A directly contradicts this theorem, making it incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: equal chords are equidistant from the center

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.3: In a circle, chords of equal length are at equal perpendicular distances from the center.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Distance from center to chord means perpendicular distance. Equal chords imply equal distances; conversely, equal distances imply equal chord lengths.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two chords are the same length, they sit the same distance away from the circle's middle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given \(AB = CD\), with perpendiculars \(OP \perp AB\) and \(OQ \perp CD\), then \(OP = OQ\). This follows from RHS congruence of triangles \(AOP\) and \(COQ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using right triangles with hypotenuses equal to radius and half-chord lengths: \((\frac{AB}{2})^2 + OP^2 = r^2\) and same for \(CD\). Since \(AB=CD\), solving gives \(OP=OQ\).
reasoning_steps: Draw perpendiculars from \(O\) to each chord.
Form right triangles with half-chords and radii.
Apply Pythagoras: equal chords → equal perpendicular distances.
misconceptions: Some think longer chords are always closer — actually shorter chords are farther.
Confusing distance along the chord with perpendicular distance.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Equal chords ↔ equal distances from center.

glossary_entry.definition: Chords of equal length in a circle are equidistant from the center.

flashcard.front: If two chords in a circle have equal length, what can you say about their distances from the center?

visual_system.visual_description: Circle center O, two equal chords AB and CD, perpendiculars OP and OQ equal length.
visual_system.caption: Equal chords have equal perpendicular distances from center.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle O, chords AB and CD equal, OP ⟂ AB, OQ ⟂ CD, OP = OQ.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does Theorem 5.3 state about equal chords?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They are equidistant from the circle's center.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Chords the same distance from the center have equal lengths.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Prove via RHS triangles or Pythagoras.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply symmetrical properties to solve problems
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Equal chords are equidistant from center (Theorem 5.3).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A central angle \(\theta\) and an inscribed angle \(a\) intercept the same arc. What is the relationship between \(\theta\) and \(a\)?
questions.hint: Theorem 5.6: inscribed angle = half central angle (same arc).
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.6 states the measure of an inscribed angle is half the measure of its intercepted central angle, so \(θ = 2a\). Option C reverses the relationship incorrectly.

source_trace.content_anchor: inscribed angle is half of central angle intercepting same arc

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.6: For the same intercepted arc, an inscribed angle measures half the central angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The central angle's vertex at center, inscribed angle's vertex on circle. The inscribed angle is always exactly half the central angle subtending the same arc.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you see an angle sitting on the circle edge, it's half as big as the angle from the center pointing to the same arc.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given arc \(AB\), central angle \(AOB = \theta\) and inscribed angle \(APB = a\), then \(\theta = 2a\). Proof uses isosceles triangles and exterior angle theorem.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Three cases (diameter on one ray, between rays, outside rays) all yield \(2a = \theta\) using properties of isosceles triangles and supplementary angles.
reasoning_steps: Identify central angle \(AOB\) and inscribed angle \(APB\).
Apply isosceles triangle property and exterior angle theorem.
misconceptions: Some think inscribed angle equals central angle — it's half.
Forgetting the arc must be the same for both angles.

glossary_entry.definition: An inscribed angle is half the measure of its intercepted central angle.

flashcard.front: How does an inscribed angle compare to its intercepted central angle?
flashcard.back: The inscribed angle is half the measure of the central angle.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle center O, points A,B,P on circle; central angle AOB, inscribed angle APB.
visual_system.caption: Central angle is twice inscribed angle subtending same arc.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle O, AOB central angle 100°, APB inscribed angle 50°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is Theorem 5.6 in circle geometry?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: An inscribed angle is half the measure of its intercepted central angle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The measure of an inscribed angle is one-half the measure of its intercepted arc (or central angle).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Prove three cases: diameter on one ray, between rays, outside rays.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write angle properties of circles in own words

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: An angle inscribed in a semicircle always measures how many degrees?
questions.hint: Thales' theorem: angle in a semicircle is a right angle.
questions.explanation: Thales' theorem states an angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle (\(90^\circ\)). Option D is a straight angle, which would mean the chord is a diameter but vertex at center, not on circle.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Theorem 5.7 Angle in a semicircle (Thales' Theorem)
source_trace.content_anchor: angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle

explanations_tiered.definition: Thales' theorem: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle (\(90^\circ\)).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the endpoints of an inscribed angle lie on a diameter, the angle is always \(90^\circ\). The intercepted arc is \(180^\circ\), so half is \(90^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you draw a triangle inside a circle so one side is the diameter, the corner opposite that side is always a perfect square corner — \(90^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given diameter \(AB\) and point \(P\) on the circle, \(\angle APB = 90^\circ\). Proof: central angle \(AOB = 180^\circ\), inscribed angle \(APB = \frac{1}{2} \times 180^\circ = 90^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Theorem 5.6, central angle subtended by a semicircle is \(180^\circ\), thus inscribed angle = \(90^\circ\). Converse also holds: right angle subtends a diameter.
reasoning_steps: Identify the arc as a semicircle (180°).
Recall inscribed angle = half intercepted arc.
misconceptions: Some think any inscribed angle is \(90^\circ\) — only when it subtends a diameter.
Confusing with central angle of \(180^\circ\) being straight line.

glossary_entry.definition: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle.

flashcard.front: What is the measure of an angle inscribed in a semicircle?

visual_system.visual_description: Circle with diameter AB, point P on circle, angle APB = 90°.
visual_system.caption: Inscribed angle in a semicircle is a right angle.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle, diameter AB horizontal, point P above, angle APB 90°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is Thales' theorem in circle geometry?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is always a right angle (90°).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: If A, B, P are points on a circle with AB as diameter, then ∠APB = 90°.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply angle properties of circles to solve problems

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors
questions.question: A circle has radius \(r\) and a central angle \(\theta\) (in degrees) subtends an arc. What is the correct formula for the arc length \(s\)?
questions.explanation: Arc length formula is \(s = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180^\circ}\) because circumference \(2\pi r\) multiplied by \(\frac{\theta}{360^\circ} = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180^\circ}\). Option C gives area of sector, not arc length.

source_trace.section_anchor: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Arc length is the distance along the curved line of a circle between two points, proportional to the central angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Arc length is a fraction of the circumference: \(\frac{\theta}{360^\circ} \times 2\pi r = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180^\circ}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you cut a pizza slice, the crust length is the arc length. Bigger angle → longer crust.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For radius \(r\) and angle \(\theta\) in degrees, arc length \(s = \frac{\theta}{360^\circ} \times 2\pi r = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180^\circ}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: In radians, \(s = r\theta\). Conversion: \(\theta_{rad} = \frac{\pi}{180^\circ} \theta_{deg}\), so \(s = r \cdot \frac{\pi \theta}{180^\circ}\).
misconceptions: Students often use area formula for arc length.
Forgetting to convert angle correctly if given in radians.

glossary_entry.definition: The distance along a curve of a circle between two points.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for arc length in degrees?

visual_system.visual_description: Circle radius r, central angle θ, arc length s highlighted.
visual_system.caption: Arc length s is proportional to θ.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with radius r, central angle θ, curved arc length s.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate arc length given radius and central angle in degrees?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The measure of a portion of the circumference of a circle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to fraction of whole circumference.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate arc length, perimeter of sectors

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors
questions.question: A chord in a circle of radius \(6\,\mathrm{cm}\) subtends a central angle of \(120^\circ\). What is the length of the chord?
questions.explanation: Chord length formula \(2r\sin(\theta/2)\) with \(r=6\), \(\theta=120^\circ\) gives \(12\sin60^\circ = 12 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 6\sqrt{3}\,\mathrm{cm}\). Option B uses \(\sin45^\circ\) incorrectly.

source_trace.section_anchor: Arc Lengths, Perimeters and Areas of Segments and Sectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Chord length is the straight-line distance between two points on a circle, given by \(2r\sin(\theta/2)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chord length depends on radius and central angle. The formula derives from isosceles triangle geometry: split into two right triangles with angle θ/2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw a pizza slice, cut it down the middle. The straight line across the crust is the chord. Its length = 2 × radius × sin(half the slice angle).
explanations_tiered.advanced: In triangle OAB with OA=OB=r, chord AB = \(2r\sin(\theta/2)\) because altitude bisects θ and opposite side = \(2r\sin(\theta/2)\).
reasoning_steps: Identify radius r and central angle θ.

formula_analysis.name: Chord length formula
formula_analysis.derivation: From right triangle with hypotenuse r and opposite side c/2, angle θ/2.

misconceptions: Using \(\sin\theta\) instead of \(\sin(\theta/2)\).
Forgetting to double the product: \(r\sin(\theta/2)\) is half the chord, not full chord.

glossary_entry.definition: The straight-line distance between two points on a circle.

flashcard.front: What is the length of a chord in a circle of radius \(6\,\mathrm{cm}\) with central angle \(120^\circ\)?

visual_system.visual_description: Circle radius 6 cm, central angle 120°, chord length shown.
visual_system.caption: Chord length = \(6\sqrt{3}\) cm.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle radius 6 cm, central angle 120°, chord length 10.39 cm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find chord length: radius 6 cm, central angle 120°
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A line segment whose endpoints lie on the circle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ensure students know θ in degrees vs radians.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle
questions.question: Two chords \(PR\) and \(QS\) intersect inside a circle at \(T\). If \(\text{m}(\text{arc}PQ) = 68^\circ\) and \(\text{m}(\text{arc}RS) = 128^\circ\), what is \(\text{m}(\angle RTS)\)?
questions.hint: Intersecting chords: angle = half sum of intercepted arcs.
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.10: \(m(\angle RTS) = \frac{1}{2}[\text{m}(\text{arc}PQ) + \text{m}(\text{arc}RS)] = \frac{1}{2}(68 + 128) = 98^\circ\). Option D is the sum without halving.

source_trace.section_anchor: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.10: When two chords intersect inside a circle, the angle formed equals half the sum of the measures of the arcs intercepted by the angle and its vertical angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The angle between intersecting chords depends on the two arcs across from it, not the arcs adjacent to it.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When two chords cross inside a circle, the angle at the crossing is the average of the two opposite arcs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proof: Draw chord QR. Using inscribed angles: \(\angle RTS = \angle TRQ + \angle TQR = \frac{1}{2}\text{m}(\text{arc}RS) + \frac{1}{2}\text{m}(\text{arc}PQ) = \frac{1}{2}(\text{arc}PQ + \text{arc}RS)\).
reasoning_steps: Sum the two arcs that are not adjacent to the angle.

formula_analysis.name: Intersecting chords angle theorem
formula_analysis.derivation: From sum of two inscribed angles.

misconceptions: Students often add the adjacent arcs instead of opposite arcs.
Forgetting to divide by 2.

glossary_entry.definition: Angle between intersecting chords equals half the sum of the measures of the opposite arcs.

flashcard.front: Two chords intersect inside circle creating angle RTS. Intercepted arcs are 68° and 128°. Find angle RTS.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle, chords PR and QS intersect at T, arcs PQ and RS marked 68° and 128°.
visual_system.caption: Angle RTS = 98°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle with intersecting chords PR and QS at T, arcs 68° and 128°, angle RTS 98°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Two chords intersect. Arcs 68° and 128° opposite the angle. Find the angle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle formed by intersecting chords = half sum of opposite arcs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply theorems on angles and arcs determined by lines intersecting circles

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle
questions.question: Two secants intersect outside a circle, intercepting a major arc of \(107^\circ\) and a minor arc of \(43^\circ\). What is the measure of the angle formed by the secants?
questions.hint: Secant-secant angle = half (far arc − near arc).
questions.explanation: Theorem 5.11: \(x = \frac{1}{2}(\text{m}(\text{arcCE}) - \text{m}(\text{arcBD})) = \frac{1}{2}(107 - 43) = 32^\circ\). Option D adds instead of subtracts before halving.

source_trace.section_anchor: Theorems on Angles and Arcs Determined by Lines Intersecting inside, on and outside a Circle

explanations_tiered.definition: Theorem 5.11: The measure of an angle formed by two secants (or tangents) intersecting outside a circle is half the difference of the measures of the intercepted arcs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Far arc minus near arc, then divided by 2. Applies to secant-secant, tangent-tangent, and secant-tangent configurations.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two lines start outside a circle and cut across, the angle between them equals half the difference between the big arc and small arc they touch.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given far arc \(107^\circ\) and near arc \(43^\circ\), \(x = \frac{1}{2}(107 - 43) = 32^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proof uses inscribed angles: draw chords to form a triangle where the external angle equals sum of two remote interior angles, each half an arc, giving the difference formula.
reasoning_steps: Identify the far arc (larger) and near arc (smaller).
Subtract near arc from far arc.

formula_analysis.name: Secant-secant angle theorem
formula_analysis.derivation: From exterior angle theorem and inscribed angles.

misconceptions: Students sometimes add arcs instead of subtracting.
Forgetting to divide by 2 after subtraction.
Using the wrong arcs (adjacent instead of intercepted).

glossary_entry.definition: Angle formed by two secants outside circle = half the difference of intercepted arcs.

flashcard.front: Two secants outside circle intersect far arc 107°, near arc 43°. Find the angle.

visual_system.visual_description: Circle, two secants from external point, far arc 107°, near arc 43°, angle 32°.
visual_system.caption: Angle = ½(107° − 43°) = 32°.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Circle, two secants from external point A, intersecting circle at B,C and D,E; arcs 107° and 43°, angle 32°.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Two secants outside circle. Far arc 107°, near arc 43°. Find the angle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A line that intersects a circle at two points.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply theorems on angles and arcs determined by lines intersecting circles
